title: Misinformation: the dangers of banning my post on seed oils

chunks.title: Introduction and Context
chunks.summary: The host introduces the topic of seed oils, specifically canola oil, and discusses the recent censorship and misinformation claims on social media regarding its potential health impacts. They emphasize the importance of open dialogue about differing opinions on nutrition.
chunks.transcript: we are in an environment where bucking the norm or talking about things that are outside of the mainstream is not tolerated and that is very dangerous on this week's podcast I'm pushing back

chunks.title: Censorship and Misinformation Claims
chunks.summary: The host recounts their experience of having a social media post labeled as misinformation and critiques the individuals and organizations that perpetuate these claims. They stress the dangers of censorship in medical discourse.

chunks.title: Critique of Experts and Studies
chunks.summary: The host criticizes certain experts whose research supports the consumption of seed oils, particularly highlighting the work of Darius Maazafarian and the controversial Food Compass guidelines. They argue that these studies exclude significant evidence showing the harm caused by seed oils.

chunks.title: Trans Fats and Processing of Canola Oil
chunks.summary: Details are discussed about the processing of canola oil, including the formation of trans fats and the presence of potential neurotoxins like hexane. The implications of these factors on health outcomes are examined.

chunks.title: Health Risks of Oxidized LDL
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the health impacts of oxidized LDL and its association with cardiovascular diseases. The host highlights studies linking seed oil consumption to increased oxidized LDL levels.
chunks.transcript: canola oil contains between 1.9 and 3.6 percent trans fatty acids which are formed in the process of heating and hydrogenation and oxidation and this is a pretty significant level as I've spoken about on social media when oils like canola or soybean oil say zero trans fat that is not true manufacturers can technically say that if they have less than 0.5 grams of trans fat per serving well a serving of oil is usually only 14 grams a tablespoon maybe even a teaspoon so if there's less than 0.5 grams per 14 grams then they can say it's zero trans fat but if the oil is 1.9 to 3.6 trans fatty acids they're actually getting pretty close to that threshold though they're not exceeding it but do you want to eat an oil that's 1.9 to 3.6 trans fatty acids definitely not if those are trans fatty acids from vegetables I'll pause here for one moment and say there are trans fatty acids in animal Foods conjugated linoleic acid a completely different trans fat than you will find when linoleic acid becomes a trans fat in the heating process so conjugated linoleic acid is a different fatty acid depends on where the trans hydrogen atoms are in the molecule conjugated linoleic acid and other trans fatty acids and animal foods have actually been associated with health improvements while we know very clearly I don't think anyone would debate this and in fact Ansel Keyes himself was the one who discovered that trans fatty acids raise LDL and further research not necessarily by him but by others clearly strongly Associated trans fatty acids from vegetable and plant oils with severely bad Health outcomes it appears that trans fatty acids formed from the

chunks.title: Studies Supporting Seed Oils
chunks.summary: The host discusses studies and claims from health experts stating that seed oils can be beneficial under certain conditions, challenging the validity of these statements with counter-evidence from significant research.

chunks.title: Closing Remarks and Call for Open Discourse
chunks.summary: The host concludes by reiterating the need for open and respectful discourse in nutritional discussions, emphasizing that many health claims about seed oils are nuanced. They encourage listeners to think critically and base their decisions on a wide range of evidence.
